questions.prompt: Which patterns in stock returns are NOT explained by CAPM but ARE captured by factor models?
choices: Small-cap stocks outperform large-cap on a risk-adjusted basis
Value stocks outperform growth stocks at similar beta
Treasury bills earn the risk-free rate
questions.explanation: Size, value, and momentum are the three classic CAPM anomalies. T-bills earning R_f is exactly what CAPM predicts.

questions.prompt: The Fama-French three-factor model adds which two factors to CAPM?
choices: SMB (Small Minus Big) and HML (High Minus Low book-to-market)
questions.explanation: SMB captures the size premium; HML captures the value premium. RMW and CMA are added in the five-factor extension.

questions.prompt: Which form of the Efficient Markets Hypothesis is most clearly violated empirically?
choices: Strong form (prices reflect all information, including insider info)
questions.explanation: Insider trading produces abnormal returns — that's why it's illegal. So strong form is clearly false. Semi-strong is mostly supported; weak form is essentially supported with momentum as a minor exception.

questions.prompt: Most active mutual fund managers underperform passive index funds after fees. What does this support?
choices: Markets are inefficient and managers are incompetent
Semi-strong EMH: even with skill, the cost of trying to beat the market typically exceeds the alpha generated
That hedge funds are better than mutual funds
questions.explanation: Persistent underperformance net of fees is exactly what semi-strong EMH predicts: competition drives alpha to zero on average, and trading costs / fees push net returns below the index.

furtherReading.title: Cliff Asness: 'The Problem Was Never Beta. The Problem Was Paying Alpha Fees for Beta'
furtherReading.why: Cliff Asness, founder of factor-investing pioneer AQR, defends factor models against years of underperformance — a real debate among practitioners, not a textbook framing.
